items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
items.correct: Graphite has delocalised electrons between layers; diamond has all four outer electrons in localised covalent bonds.
items.misconception: Graphite conducts because it is a metal, while diamond is a non-metal.
items.random_wrong: Graphite conducts because it can melt easily at room temperature.
items.why: Students associate 'conducts electricity' with 'is a metal' and don't recognise that some non-metallic allotropes have free electrons too.

items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: Which type of substance has a LOW melting point and does NOT conduct electricity?
items.why: It's Simple molecular. “Giant ionic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is NOT an allotrope of carbon?
items.why: Correct answer: Silica (SiO₂). “Diamond” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Diamond's melting point is about 3550 °C. Methane (simple molecular) melts at about −182 °C. What's the difference, in °C?
items.why: The real figure is 3732. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
